content.claim: School starting age (a mechanically related but distinct selection intervention) produces counter-intuitive equity effects: children gain from starting school one year later, and effects are substantially stronger for children from low-educated families, attributed to kindergarten's superior efficacy at developing low-status children's skills relative to schools.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Equity and social mobility lens
limitations: Concerns delayed school entry rather than gimnázium tracking directly
Suggests that not all 'early selection' mechanisms produce uniform equity effects across different policy instruments
Finding is from single research lineage; generalizability to age-10/12 gimnázium entry unclear
Mechanism (kindergarten vs. school quality) may not transfer to intermediate-level tracking context
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
